events.title: FOMC holds rates steady in March 2026; dot plot converges on one cut for remainder of year
events.summary: The March 2026 FOMC meeting (concluded March 18) kept the federal funds rate at 3.5%-3.75%. The dot plot and market-implied probabilities now converge on at most one additional cut in 2026, a hawkish shift from earlier expectations of two cuts. Core PCE rose to 3.1% in January, reinforcing the Fed's caution. The market has repriced from ~2 cuts to ~1 cut for the rest of the year.
events.why_now: The March FOMC statement and press conference are the most recent policy signal. The repricing from two expected cuts to one is a meaningful tightening of financial conditions expectations that would ripple through equity valuations, bond yields, and mortgage rates within the March 31 window.
events.market_relevance: Fewer rate cuts than previously expected tighten financial conditions at the margin. This pressures rate-sensitive sectors (REITs, utilities, homebuilders, growth/tech), supports bank net interest margins, and keeps mortgage rates elevated, dampening housing affordability and transaction volumes.

events.title: February 2026 non-farm payrolls show unexpected loss of 92,000 jobs
events.summary: The February 2026 jobs report showed the U.S. economy unexpectedly lost 92,000 jobs versus an expected gain of 55,000. Unemployment edged up to 4.4%. This is the weakest labor print in the current cycle and signals a potential inflection toward recession-like labor dynamics.
events.why_now: This data was released in early March and is the freshest hard economic data informing market positioning on March 31. The magnitude of the miss (negative vs. positive consensus) is a significant shock that raises recession probability estimates.
events.market_relevance: A sharply negative payrolls print raises recession risk, which is bearish for cyclical equities, consumer discretionary, and credit spreads. It is also bearish for commercial real estate occupancy and rent growth. However, it creates a tug-of-war with the Fed's inflation concerns, limiting the policy response.

events.title: War in Iran drives energy price spike and inflation uncertainty
events.summary: A Middle East conflict involving Iran is referenced as an active geopolitical event affecting energy prices and inflation expectations. The TreasurySpring analysis notes the war's pass-through effects on inflation, consumer spending, and growth are the key unknown for the FOMC and markets.
events.why_now: The conflict appears to have escalated in the weeks before March 31, 2026. Energy price shocks feed into CPI within 1-2 months and immediately affect consumer sentiment, transportation costs, and corporate margins.
events.market_relevance: Energy price spikes benefit oil & gas producers but hurt airlines, logistics, consumer discretionary, and any energy-intensive sector. For real estate, higher energy costs raise operating expenses for commercial properties and reduce consumer purchasing power for housing. The inflation impulse also reduces the probability of Fed rate cuts, keeping mortgage rates elevated.

events.title: Fed leadership transition uncertainty: Powell vs. Warsh nomination battle
events.summary: Fed Chair Jay Powell is pledging to stay until a DoJ investigation concludes or is dismissed. Senator Thom Tillis is attempting to block the nomination of Kevin Warsh as Powell's successor. Warsh, historically hawkish, has recently adopted a more dovish stance aligned with President Trump's preference for rate cuts. The leadership outcome could shift the FOMC's policy direction materially.
events.why_now: This is an active political and institutional drama as of the March 18 FOMC press conference. The uncertainty over who will lead the Fed creates a policy volatility premium that markets must price on March 31.
events.market_relevance: Fed chair transitions historically create volatility in rates markets. A Warsh appointment with dovish leanings could lead to faster rate cuts, boosting rate-sensitive assets. Continued Powell tenure maintains the current cautious approach. The uncertainty itself is a headwind for long-duration positioning.

events.title: Core PCE inflation rises to 3.1%, well above Fed's 2% target
events.summary: Core PCE inflation rose for a second consecutive month in January 2026 to 3.1%, confirming that inflation remains sticky and well above the Fed's 2% target. This is occurring even before the full pass-through of tariffs and the Iran conflict's energy price effects.
events.why_now: The January core PCE reading was highlighted in the February FOMC minutes and is the most recent inflation data shaping market expectations. With tariff effects still feeding through and energy prices rising from the Iran conflict, the inflation trajectory is worsening.
events.market_relevance: Persistent above-target inflation constrains the Fed's ability to cut rates even as the labor market weakens, creating a stagflationary dynamic. This is negative for bonds (higher real yields), growth stocks (higher discount rates), and real estate (higher cap rates and mortgage rates). TIPS and commodities may benefit.

events.title: Tariff effects still feeding through to prices, compounding inflation
events.summary: TreasurySpring notes that the full effect of tariffs has not yet been reflected in inflation data, meaning core PCE at 3.1% may understate the true inflationary impulse. This adds another layer of upward price pressure on top of the Iran-related energy shock.
events.why_now: Tariff pass-through operates on a lag of several months. As of March 31, 2026, the cumulative effect is still building, making forward inflation expectations increasingly uncertain and complicating Fed policy.
events.market_relevance: Tariff-driven cost increases compress margins for importers, retailers, and manufacturers reliant on global supply chains. They also raise input costs for construction materials, directly affecting housing supply costs and commercial real estate development economics.

events.title: 10-year Treasury yield expected to remain in 3.75%-4.25% range
events.summary: LPL Research forecasts the 10-year Treasury yield to hover between 3.75% and 4.25% in 2026, reflecting a rangebound rate environment as the Fed maintains a 'slightly restrictive' stance. This range anchors mortgage rates and discount rates for asset valuations.
events.why_now: With the March FOMC confirming the higher-for-longer stance and inflation remaining sticky, this yield range is being validated by market pricing as of March 31.
events.market_relevance: A 10-year yield in the 3.75%-4.25% range keeps 30-year mortgage rates likely in the 6.5%-7.0% range, constraining housing demand and refinancing. For equities, it maintains pressure on long-duration growth stocks while supporting financial sector earnings. For commercial real estate, it keeps cap rates elevated and transaction volumes subdued.
